coredata.dc:title: Rhodium/silicon quantum dot/carbon quantum dot composites as highly efficient electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction with Pt-like performance
coredata.dc:creator: Dang, Qian
Liao, Fan
Sun, Yuyang
Zhang, Shanshan
Huang, Hui
Shen, Wen
Kang, Zhenhui
Shi, Yandi
Shao, Mingwang
coredata.dc:description: Though noble metals play a key role in the clean energy research, their wide practical applications have been limited by the high cost and the scarce natural reserves. It is significant to decrease their usage and increase the active sites in the catalysis. Herein, Rh nanocrystals were grown on the surface of silicon quantum dots with the help of SiH bonds to obtain RhSi composites, which combined with carbon quantum dots to fabricate RhSiC nanocomposites. The silicon quantum dots limited the size of Rh and prevented the aggregation. The addition of carbon quantum dots greatly increased the electron transportation during the catalytic process. When the RhSiC nanocomposites were applied as electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the optimal one exhibits Pt-like HER performance (low Tafel slope of 26 mV·dec-1 and long-term stability) with the content of noble metal less than half of that in 20 wt% Pt/C. This work could put forward an effective avenue to develop high efficient and affordable electrochemical catalysts for HER.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalysis
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Rhodium
Silicon
Carbon

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of the fabrication of Rh/SiQD/CQD nanocomposites.
(A) XRD patterns of Rh/SiQD, SiQD/CQD, and Rh/SiQD/CQD-3; (B) FTIR spectra of SiQD, CQD, SiQD/CQD, Rh/SiQD, Rh/SiQD/CQD-3; and high-resolution XPS spectra of (C) C 1s and (D) Rh 3d of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3.

(A) TEM image of SiQDs; (B) HRTEM image of Rh/SiQD; (C) HRTEM image of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3; (D) SAED pattern of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3; and (E) and (F) HAADF-STEM image of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and corresponding EDS mapping showing C (red), Si (yellow), and Rh (indigo). The insets in (A) show the size distribution of the SiQDs and HRTEM of SiQD. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) LSV curves of different Rh/SiQD/CQD catalysts; (B) LSV curves of CQD/SiQD, Rh/SiQD, Rh/SiQD/CQD-3, Rh/CQD and 20 wt% Pt/C; and (C) the corresponding Tafel plots derived from (B); and (D) the mass activity of Rh/SiQD/CQD and 20 wt% Pt/C catalysts.
(A) EIS: Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and Rh/SiQD; and (B) Chronopotential curve of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 at current density of 10 mA cm−2, showing the better stability of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 than that of 20% Pt/C catalyst.
A schematic representation of the HER reaction mechanism.

head.ce:title: Rhodium/silicon quantum dot/carbon quantum dot composites as highly efficient electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction with Pt-like performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Though noble metals play a key role in the clean energy research, their wide practical applications have been limited by the high cost and the scarce natural reserves. It is significant to decrease their usage and increase the active sites in the catalysis. Herein, Rh nanocrystals were grown on the surface of silicon quantum dots with the help of SiH bonds to obtain RhSi composites, which combined with carbon quantum dots to fabricate RhSiC nanocomposites. The silicon quantum dots limited the size of Rh and prevented the aggregation. The addition of carbon quantum dots greatly increased the electron transportation during the catalytic process. When the RhSiC nanocomposites were applied as electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the optimal one exhibits Pt-like HER performance (low Tafel slope of 26 mV·dec-1 and long-term stability) with the content of noble metal less than half of that in 20 wt% Pt/C. This work could put forward an effective avenue to develop high efficient and affordable electrochemical catalysts for HER.

Rhodium/silicon quantum dot/carbon quantum dot nanocomposites are constructed with the help of SiH bonds. The silicon quantum dots limit the size of Rh and prevent the aggregation. The addition of carbon quantum dots greatly increased the electron transportation. The as-prepared catalysts show high mass activity and long-term stability towards hydrogen evolution reaction.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing environmental concerns and energy dilemma, scientists have paid much attention to the advanced technologies for clean and renewable energy sources [1–4]. Hydrogen is a promising candidate with high energy density, which can be produced by water splitting with electrocatalysts [5–9].

Noble metal catalysts are considered to be the best electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [10–12]. However, the high cost and scarcity of noble metals have limited their application. So it is significant to reduce the loading of noble metals in the catalysts. One strategy is to use nanotechnology because nanomaterials have vast surface area and may supply numerous active sites for catalysts. However, pure nanomaterials without proper support may cause serious aggregation during the catalytic process. So choosing proper supports, such as carbon [13–15], graphene and oxides [16,17], with large surface area and strong binding force to combine the catalysts closely and prevent them from agglomeration is a commonly used method.

Among various support materials, hydrogen-terminated silicon quantum dots (SiQD) has attracted our concern [18,19]. They are hydrophilic and have large surface area. Due to the reduction property of SiH bonds, small sized noble metal nanoparticles can be directly reduced and anchored on the surface of SiQDs. Our group has synthesized noble metal nanoparticles decorated SiNWs [20,21], which exhibited good electrocatalytic performance. Yet, the electrical conductivity of SiNWs is not good. And we also want to know if the zero-dimensional materials may show different property in electrocatalysis. So another zero-dimensional material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is introduced.

CQDs have various surface functional groups, which is very stable in acid solution. Moreover, the different kinds of functional groups would play important roles in the catalytic reaction via enhance the adsorption of intermediates and accelerate the electron transport [22–25].

Here, we fabricate Rh/SiQD/CQD nanocomposites by reducing metal salts with H-terminated SiQDs at room temperature. Notably, adding CQDs could improve the stability and activity of catalysts effectively. Moreover, the optimal Rh/SiQD/CQD catalyst showed great electrocatalytic activity, with low Tafel slope (26 mV·dec−1), good stability in acid solution, and large apparent exchange current density. Such good performance may owe to the synergy among Rh NPs, SiQDs and CQDs.

Silicon wafer (p-type, 0.008 Ω sensitivity, and single polish) was from Lijin Technology Ltd. The RhCl3·3H2O was supported by Aladdin Industrial Co. Nafion (5 wt %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. The commercial Pt/C catalyst was from Alfa Aesar Co. (Platinum, nominally 20 wt% on carbon black). Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade without further purification. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

H-terminated SiQDs were synthesized by an electrolytic etching method [23]. Briefly, Si wafers were cleaned in 40% hydrofluoric acid (HF), ethanol and acetone orderly to remove surface oxides and impurities. The electrolyte of the electrochemical etching process was prepared by mixing 40 mL of ethanol/HF (3:2) with 15 mL H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide 30%) as the electrolyte. Then the electrolyte solution was covered by suitable amount of n-hexane, preventing its further oxidation during the etching process. The electrochemical system consisted of two Si wafers (1 cm × 2 cm) both as anode and cathode. The electrolytic current ranged from 10 to 20 mA and continued for 5 h, which was supplied by a direct-current power. Finally, the Si wafer from anode was put into 10 mL ethanol with continuously ultrasonic treatment for 1 h to obtain the SiQDs.

The CQDs were synthesized by a reported electrochemical method [20]. Two pure graphite rods were cleaned in deionized water with ultrasonic processing. Then, 30 V was applied though the electrodes using a direct current power supply with continually magnetic stirring. After ten days, originally colorless electrolyte changed to a dark solution gradually. Then the dark solution was filtrated.

Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 catalyst was prepared via the following procedure. 90 μL Rh3+ (4 mg/mL) and 300 μL SiQDs were added into a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube. After that, 600 μL CQDs solution were added into the mixture with continuous ultrasonic treatment for 5 h. Then, the formed catalysts were washed with ethanol for three times. Other catalysts were prepared in a similar way with different starting materials, which were listed in Table S1 in Support Materials.

Photoluminescence (PL) study was recorded on a Fluorolog-TCSPC Luminescence Spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (Philips X'pert PRO MPD diffractometer) with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were characterized by Microscopic Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer. The content of Rh in catalysts was determined by ICPMS and ratio of different substances was studied by EDS using Zeiss G500 scanning electron microscope (Gemini 500). The morphology and structure of catalysts were characterized by Transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20), high resolution TEM (HRTEM), and elemental mapping with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. The chemical state of catalysts was studied using the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD Ultrahigh vacuum surface analysis system.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out by the CHI 760 D electrochemical workstation with a three electrode system. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode and a carbon rod as the counter electrode. The working electrode was the modified glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter). To prepared working electrode, 1.3 mg catalyst was dispersed in 1.2 mL water-ethyl alcohol solution. Then, 0.10 mL Nafion solution (0.5 wt%) was added and stirred to form a homogeneous suspension by ultrasonic irradiation. 5 μL of the suspension was loaded on the GCE. The different loadings of noble metal on the working electrode were listed in Table S2 in Support Materials.

Fig. 1 illustrates the formation of Rh/SiQD/CQD nanocomposites. The fabrication was conducted ultrasonically in a mixture of SiQD, CQDs and Rh3+ aqueous solution. The SiQDs were synthesized by an electrolytic etching method [18]. The poluminescence (PL) spectrum and the corresponding photograph under UV light of SiQDs are shown in Fig. S1. The synthesized H-terminated SiQDs contains much SiH bonds, through which the Rh3+ was reduced to Rh atoms then in-situ grow into nanoparticles. Then the CQDs were induced and attached on the Rh/SiQD, which can further keep the nanoparticle from aggregation due to the abundant surface functional groups of CQDs.

The as-prepared SiQD/CQD, Rh/SiQD, Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 were characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2 A. There is no obvious diffraction peak of SiQD/CQD in the XRD pattern, which may be due to their amorphous structure and low contents. The peaks located at 2θ values of 41° in curves for Rh/SiQD and Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 can be assigned to the (111) crystal plane of cubic Rh (JCDPS No. 05-0685). The results provide the evidence that Rh was reduced by hydrogen-terminated SiQDs.

FTIR spectra of CQDs, SiQDs, SiQD/CQD and Rh/SiQD are shown in Fig. 2B. The FTIR spectrum of CQD (black curve) demonstrates that the prepared CQDs only have simple functional groups. The peak around 1630 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of CC/CO bonds. The bands at 3440 and 1040 cm−1 are assigned to COH and COC, respectively. For SiQDs (green curve), the peaks at around 630 cm−1 and 2100 cm−1 are attributed to rocking vibration and stretching vibration of SiH bonds [26]. Compared to the pure CQDs and pure SiQDs, the SiQD/CQD composite didn't show any difference, indicating the SiH would not be destroyed by adding of CQDs. However, the Rh/SiQD and Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 composite showed the disappearance of SiH peak, because of the reduction of Rh3+. The spectrum of SiQDs shows the existence of SiO peak at 1095 cm−1 due to the partly oxidation, which may form hydrogen bonds with the COOH or COH bonds on the surface of CQDs under ultrasonic treatment.

The chemical composition and electronic structure of the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 catalyst were further investigated by XPS. The full scanning XPS spectrum of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 in Fig. S2 clearly shows the existence of C, O, Si and Rh elements. There is also In peak because it was employed as the sample holder in the XPS experiment. The high resolution C1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 C, which comes from the CQDs. The peak at 284.6 is assigned to the CC/CC of the graphite structure of CQDs, and the peaks located at 286.3 and 288.2 eV are assigned to CO and CO, respectively [27]. The high resolution Rh 3d spectrum (Fig. 2D) may be divided as Rh0 at 307.2 eV (3d5/2) and 312.0 eV (3d3/2) and Rh3+ at 308.2 eV (3d5/2) and 313.0 eV (3d3/2) respectively, indicating that most of the Rh3+ was reduced to metallic Rh by H-terminated SiQDs.

The morphology and structure of SiQDs, CQDs, Rh/SiQD, and Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 3A, the TEM image confirms that SiQDs exhibits fine dispersity with diameters of 1–4 nm. The HRTEM image of a SiQD (inset, Fig. 3A) shows the lattice fringe spacing of 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of Si crystal (JCDPS No. 27-1402). An HRTEM image of the Rh/SiQD is shown in Fig. 3B. In addition to the lattice fringe spacing of 0.31 nm of Si (111) plane, the spacing of 0.22 nm may be indexed as (111) plane of Rh (JCDPS No. 05-0685). This image clearly shows that Rh and Si are tightly combined, indicating that Rh NCs were in-situ grown on the surface of SiQDs. Fig. 3C displays an HRTEM image of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 composite, which clearly shows the firmly contact between Rh, SiQDs and CQDs. Moreover, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.31, 0.34 and 0.22 nm may be indexed as Si (111), C (002) and Rh (111) planes. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 (Fig. 3D) also confirms the existence of Rh NCs. The diffraction rings of Si or C are too weak to be seen. The EDS spectrum form SEM image (Fig. S3) of the sample shows no peak of other elements except Si, C, Rh, Cu and O, which derived from Rh NPs, SiQDs, CQDs, Cu wafer and oxygen adsorbed on the samples. Fig. 3E shows the HAADF-STEM image of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3. The corresponding EDS mapping (Fig. 3F) reveals the elemental distributions of carbon (red), rhodium (indigo) and silicon (yellow), confirming the relation of Rh NCs, SiQDs and CQDs. And the overlap of C, Rh and Si is shown in Fig. S4.

To explore the performance of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3, a series of electrocatalytic experiment were carried out. The HER electrocatalytic activities of Rh/SiQD/CQD catalysts (Fig. 4 A) were investigated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 at a sweep rate of 5 mV/s. The HER activity increases from Rh/SiQD/CQD-1 to Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and then decreases from Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 to Rh/SiQD/CQD-5, with Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 being the optimal catalyst. The decreased performance of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 to Rh/SiQD/CQD-5 may be due to the cohesion of Rh particles that results in a reduced number of active sites with the increase of Rh content. And we change the C/Si ratio keeping the content of Rh constant as Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 (Fig. S5). The HER activity decreased along with the decreased C/Si ratio, which may be due to the addition of CQDs improving the conductivity of the catalysts.

The comparison of catalytic activities among Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and other catalysts was conducted and shown in Fig. 4B. The Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 exhibited better catalytic activity than pure Rh/SiQD or Rh/CQD catalysts. The current density of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 even surpassed that of the 20% Pt/C at high overpotentials. It is interesting to note that Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 exhibited a lower overpotential (36 mV at 10 mA cm−2) than those of Rh/SiQD (65 mV at 10 mA cm−2) and Rh/CQD (176 mV at 10 mA cm−2), which indicated that the composition of three materials has synergistic effect and can effectively improve the HER performances.

The Tafel plots of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3, Rh/SiQD, 20 wt% Pt/C and Rh/CQD were derived from the polarization curves and shown in Fig. 4C. These plots were fitted with the equation (ƞ = a + b Log (-j)). The observed Tafel slope of 26.0 mV·dec−1 for Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 in this experiment is smaller than that of commercial 20 wt% Pt/C catalysts (29.3 mV·dec−1).

In HER mechanism, it is supported that there are two possible steps in acid media [28]. The first step is Volmer reaction, in which H3O+ adsorb on the surface of electrodes and accept electron to form Had. The second step is a Heyrovsky reaction (b = 40 mV·dec−1), or a Tafel reaction (b = 30 mV·dec−1), in which Had desorb on the active sites to form hydrogen. The force between Had with active sites would influence the reaction rate. Corresponding rate determining step is Tafel reaction, and a small Tafel slope could manifest a fast reaction rate of HER, which reveals the superior HER performances of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3.

The exchange current density (j
0 ) is an inherent property determined by fitting LSV data to the Tafel equation. The j
0 value of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 is 0.909 mA cm−2, which is the largest among 20% Pt/C and Rh/SiQD. The electrochemical parameters of catalysts were listed in Table S3. The mass activity of all catalysts was calculated to explain the good HER performance as shown in Fig. 4D. Due to the low content of Rh, the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 show the largest mass activity compared with other catalysts in HER, even the mass activity of Rh/SiQD/CQD is almost double that of 20% Pt/C at −0.2 V.

Hydrogen under potential deposition (HUPD) method could evaluate the active surfaces of catalysts [2]. Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 (164.7 cm2/mg) exhibits larger electrochemical active surface area than of 20 wt% Pt/C (73.1 cm2 mg−1) (Fig. S6).

To explain the high catalytic activity of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3, the electrode kinetics are charactered by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 5 A). The Nyquist plots of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and Rh/SiQD were measured in the frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. In the impedance spectra, the equivalent circuit is represented by the 2 TS model (Rs–(R1 || CPE1)–(Rct || CPEint)) [29]: Rs is the solution resistance, R1||CPE1 is a higher-frequency component corresponding to electrode surface pores, and Rct || CPEint is a lower-frequency component related to resistance of the charge-transfer step. Rct is related to the electrocatalytic kinetics. The Rct value of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 (22.29 Ω) is lower than that of Rh/SiQD (45.99 Ω), which imply the introduction of CQDs could realize fast charge transfer of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 and is in favor of HER. As shown in Fig. S7, the Nyquist plots of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 were measured at different potential in the range of −0.030 to −0.045 V. The parameters of the equivalent circuits for the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 electrocatalyst were calculated and listed in Table S4 (Supporting information). On the basis of these parameters, the Cdl and real surface area of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 electrocatalyst were determined to be 2.007 × 10−5 F cm−2 and 1.003 cm2. The detailed calculation was described in the Supporting Materials.

The stability is another significant parameter for HER, which may be measured by chronopotentiometry technique at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 (Figs. 5B and S8). During the 23 h test, the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 catalyst has a smaller potential shift than that of 20% Pt/C, indicating that Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 exhibits a good durability in acidic condition.

The pH effect of the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 was measured by the LSV technique in different concentration of H2SO4 (Fig. S9). Fig. S9B shows the equation about the cathodic current density of HER at −0.06 V vs. RHE on different H+ concentration: log j = 1.37–1.80 pH, r = −0.998, which hydrogen evolution reaction obeys second order reaction kinetics.

We propose the following mechanism to explain the excellent effect of Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 (Fig. 6 ): (i) the H+ adsorbs on the surface of Rh and forms hydrogen atoms via accepting electron transferred from CQDs; then (ii) the Rh-adsorbed hydrogen migrates to Si surface; and (iii) finally the adsorbed-H on the surface of Si reacts with hydrogen ion to form hydrogen gas.

In summary, one effective Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 electrocatalyst was synthesized with H-terminated SiQDs as a reduce agent. Rh nanoparticle in-situ grow on the surface of H-terminated SiQDs, which increase the connection between the noble metal with support, The addition of CQDs could effectively keep Rh nanoparticle from aggregation, provide anchor sites, and accelerate the charge transfer rate. The experimental results shows that the Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 exhibited excellent HER performances, which is superior to that of the 20 wt% Pt/C catalyst at high overpotential. Furthermore, chronopotentiometry technique revealed that Rh/SiQD/CQD-3 exhibited superior stability. This work will put forward an effective method for developing other high efficient electroncatalysts.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204800), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), the 111 Project, Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.01.031.
